I'm not sure dropping dev dependency versions (`pytest` here) is a good idea, or, _I assume_ that it's not an improvement, or what are the upsides? are there any?

Take the following as a comment only, by someone who previously fought with another stepper motor controller with a 400 page manual (:D), not(!) a request to remodel. I fully understand if you consider it "good enough" and just want to get this in.

In general, I feel a bit confused by the naming and API: slewing and moving relatively are methods, positions are a property; there is `slew` and `go`, which sounds pretty similar effect-wise; the sometimes slim distinction between step-the-verb (as in the method of that name) and step-the-attribute (in various args) and "step-the-noun" (in the property `steps`) vs. "position" vs `step_position`;
I think it could be clearer to have a triplet of methods like `move` (slew), `move_for` (steps, in direction), `move_to` (a position), but I understand why you want a `position` property to query. maybe add a thin wrapper method for the latter? 
Also, should `step` and `go` be `_step` and `_go`, i.e. "private" properties, to reduce the API surface a bit? Or do you envision yourself using this a lot, e.g. in preprogrammed but delayed moves?

[**edit**: this comment is actually about a different plugin that was mentioned in a related issue]

Though I'd like to include the Leaflet.Control.Layers.Tree plugin in folium, I don't think this PR is the way we should do it, for three reasons:

- The code in this PR contains a specific use case of that JS plugin: radio buttons in separate groups. The JS plugin itself doesn't only contain that, but accepts a generic layer tree.
- It contains custom code in Python as well as the JS template. Ideally, a folium plugin should only wrap the JS plugin. But maybe some is unavoidable here.
- It partially reimplements code of the normal LayerControl class.

IMO an adoption of this JS plugin should have as little code on the folium side as possible and expose the full feature. It should either replace the existing LayerControl class or be compatible with it.

I wonder how that can be done best in folium, since on the Python-side we structure layers a bit different than Leaflet. My best suggestion would be to pass a dictionary with layers to this plugin, then pass that to the JS plugin. And then not use the normal layer control. Something like:

```
layer1 = Layer()
layer2 = FeatureGroup()
layer 3 = Marker()
GroupedLayerControl(
    base_layers=[layer1],
    overlays={
        'Group': [layer2, layer3]
    }
).add_to(m)
````

Note that this mimics the arguments of the JS plugin. That means little code on the folium side. Also note that this doesn't use the `overlay` and `control` parameters of the folium objects, because the tree structure passed to GroupedLayerControl` defines how the layers are used.

I'd be interested to hear how others think about this.

I'm not a huge fan of this refactor as it is but I think it's a step in the right direction. There's too much coupling to the specific data model used by /v0.3/v0.4/v0.5 traces which we're just splitting into two classes. The parent class `HTTPWriter` still has dependence on the same logic and contains agent specific logic.

If reusability is the objective then I think a "has-a" relationship would be better than a "is-a". That is, encapsulate the transmission state and the corresponding metrics into its own class and have writers maintain an instance of that class.

This PR did separate a bit of the logic, I think we just need to pull out the last bits of agent-specific code and change the class relationship.

Awesome, massive improvement! Mostly nits and the like, but also a few other tentative suggestions:

1. I mentioned this in a comment in the review, but I think that a framing of "tensor datasets" (single-tensor-column table that presents a collection-of-tensors concept and API to the user) and "tensors in tabular datasets" (multi-column table that contains one or more tensor columns) makes more sense than the current framing of "single-column" and "multi-column".
2. Should we have a "Transforming Tensor Datasets" section that demonstrates batch transformations on tensor data? I know that it will be similar to the "Consuming Tensor Datasets" section and I know there's a call-out in that section, but not having a "how to transform tensor data" section will seem like a glaring omission when a user is scanning the docs. It would also be a nice section to add examples of how the tensor extension can be manipulated in a Pandas DataFrame as if its a native type (e.g. support arithmetic and aggregation operations and the like).
3. If we are showing how tensor datasets are formatted in batch transformations and consumption, I think that we should also have a section describing how rows for tensor datasets are presented, in the row-based transformation and consumption APIs (i.e. that tensor datasets are transparently converted to NumPy ndarrays in row-based APIs).

While it makes sense to make the code more resilient against `PermissionError`, I think it would be better to fix the root cause of the issue, namely that the `localstack/infra/...` folders within the Docker container are owned by root and not writable by other users.

Running under a non-root user currently breaks the startup/install routine, as you pointed out:
```
$ DOCKER_FLAGS='--user localstack' DEBUG=1 localstack start
...
File "/opt/code/localstack/localstack/services/install.py", line 525, in install_dynamodb_local
save_file(log4j2_file, log4j2_config)
File "/opt/code/localstack/localstack/utils/files.py", line 64, in save_file
with open(file, mode, opener=_opener if permissions else None) as f:
PermissionError: [Errno 13] Permission denied: '/opt/code/localstack/localstack/infra/dynamodb/log4j2.xml'
```

If we add the following line to our `Dockerfile`:
```
RUN chmod -R 777 localstack/infra/dynamodb
```
... then the startup works fine:
```
$ DOCKER_FLAGS='--user localstack' DEBUG=1 localstack start
...
Ready.
```

(We could apply the same chmod to the entire infra folder, not just `infra/dynamodb`)

It is a bit unclear to me if you provide an EMD, multimarginal solver, a barycenter estimator (especially since you function takes only one array as input) so please clarify this in the code and maybe the PR description.

What needs to be clarified and changed:
+ Move all solvers to a submodule better named more clear such as `ot.lp.discrete_emd` .
+ Please respect POT API: use M for ground loss, a,A for distributions (on the simplex) and all parameters names. All function names can be long but must describe precisely what the function does. For instance if a function computes a barycenter then it should be similarly named to other solvers and be called the same way.
+ Add to the documentation of all function, the optimization problems solved in math environment using same names as other POT functions.

For the sample window issue, I think we can,
1. Use receive timestamp debug to check if the start of frame is not far from the start of sample window. If it is delayed, then we should check what is the cause of the delay on CSL transmitter side, timer scheduled delay, software processing time or CSMA/CA not turned off.

Hi, here is my review. I read through all the code and briefly tested the new feature.

This review-comment has 3 parts: questions, uds, tests. Feedback to fastapi are included in my code-comments

### Questions:
1. Maybe we/you could point out the idea of the Mets-Server again (then it is better for me to recap): IIUC the idea is that all interactions with a workspace is done by the Mets-Server. So there is just one process (the server) accessing the Metsfile. What I am wondering is: when a user initiates multiple calls to the server how is it ensured that the metsfile is accessed in a "synchronized way". Can't it be for example that the Met-Server itself receives 2 requests to add a file to the mets and this calls "happen at the same time" and thus the writes collide somehow? Or is this related somehow to the mets caching?

2. 
> Processors do still need to specify the workspace directory because they read files from disk

I don't understand what you mean by this. Does it mean in addition to host/port the path to the workspace has to be specified? If so why, as far as I understand the mets_servers only needs to know.

3. 
> whether downloading from the server should be A Thing

sry, I don't understand that either, can you try to clarify?


### Unix sockets
I don't have much experience with unix sockets. What is the benefit in using sockets compared to using tcp? I am wondering if it's worth the added "complexity" because both (uds and tcp) offer nearly the same thing (iiuc). What I would do is go with tcp and drop the sockets at least for the beginning (although it seems to be nicely usable with uvicorn).


### Tests
I didn't think very much about the tests yet. I am wondering if it is time for that yet, I know about TDD but I think I am not experienced/used to do that.
But in the webapi-implementation we already have some tests regarding fastapi. There we also use pytest-docker because we need mongodb (and later probably rabbit-mq) as well. I am not sure yet weather we need this also for the metsserver. Currently I don't think so. Testing api endpoints with fastapi is in my opinion simple (using docker is a bit more complicated from my point of view). First a fixture (not mandatory but I think a fixture is the best way) for the fastapi-Instance/TestClient is needed: https://github.com/OCR-D/ocrd-webapi-implementation/blob/main/tests/conftest.py#L33-L36 and then it can be used to execute http methods: https://github.com/OCR-D/ocrd-webapi-implementation/blob/main/tests/conftest.py#L176-L177. The rest (starting the server etc) is done by fastapi.

Do you mind adding two pytest checks for this?
Is there a way to avoid "while True"?

After some more thought, I'm leaning towards deprecating the parameter. The primary reason is that the default used to be `south_at_latitude_tilt` and users had to specify `orientation_stragety=None` to prevent `ModelChain` from modifying their systems (https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/issues/290). As a long result of that bad API decision years ago, I still have modern code that sets the parameter to None. I'm guessing that at least a few other users might be in the same position.

FWIW, I briefly searched github and google for pvlib and south_at_latitude_tilt. I found one article in energies that used the parameter. I did not find any maintained code that uses it.

Thanks for being scrupulous about testing. You can add this sort of thing to `setup_py_integration_test.py` to test:

```
def test_output_dir() -> None:
    dist_dir = "dist"
    output_dir = os.path.join("nondefault", "output_dir")
    files = {
        "foo/BUILD": dedent(
            f"""\
            python_sources()
            
            python_distribution(
                name="dist",
                dependencies=[":foo"],
                provides=python_artifact(name="foo", version="2.3.4"),
                output_dir="{output_dir}"
            )
            """
        ),
        "foo/source.py": "",
    }
    pyver = f"{sys.version_info.major}.{sys.version_info.minor}.{sys.version_info.micro}"

    with setup_tmpdir(files) as source_dir:
        pants_run = run_pants(
            [
                "--backend-packages=pants.backend.python",
                f"--python-interpreter-constraints=['=={pyver}']",
                "package",
                f"{source_dir}/foo:dist",
            ],
            extra_env={"PYTHON": sys.executable},
        )
        pants_run.assert_success()
        dists = os.listdir(dist_dir)
        assert len(dists) == 2
        for dist in dists:
            assert dist.startswith(output_dir)
```

So you should make the following changes in `__init__.py`:

1. (`__init__.py`) Move this checker into the list of other custom checkers being registered.
2. (`__init__.py`) Remove the `pyta_pep8` configuration option, so that the checker is always loaded.

@StrikerRUS whenever you have time, could you give your opinion? I can give you the relevant context so you don't have to re-read all the discussion here and in #3768. 

In #3766, I added a function `_find_open_port()` that the Dask package uses to build the `machines` list. But as I noted in https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/3766#discussion_r557736104, the approach adds some overhead. It currently runs that check sequentially. So for a 3-worker cluster, it will go "find port on `w1` then find port on `w2` then find port on `w3`". This means there is O(n_workers) overhead introduced.

This PR proposes running a function to find a random port on every worker simultaneously, so in theory the overhead of setting up `machines` becomes O(1). This is faster, but it also means the ports used for `machines` are totally unrelated to `local_listen_port`.

In https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/3823#issuecomment-768816075 I told @jmoralez that in my opinion, this faster behavior should be the default, but that we need to still give people who are limited by firewall rules the ability to specify a `local_listen_port` and know that only that port and those near it will be searched.

So we settled on the current state of this PR, which is:

1. if local_listen_port is missing from parameters, use the faster approach
1. if local_listen_port is set to 12400 (LightGBM's default), use the faster approach
1. if local_listen_port is set to a value other than 12400, use the `_find_open_port()` approach where we specifically search 1000 ports from `local_listen_port` to `local_listen_port + 999`.

I'd suggest making the changes to `changes.rst` outside of the scope of adding indexes on `history_date` in a new PR.

I don't think the fallbacks to hardlinking and copying are quite the right approach. As it it looks like we're going to try and fail to chmod every owner-executable file that is linked in as an import and owned by someone else.

We could only do the chmod if we actually own the ultimate target of any links. And if we don't own the target, and we want it to be executable, we should only make a copy if it isn't actually executable by us already.

A few additional thoughts besides the comments in the review.
About registration of functions, it is not unusual in some web contexts (for example routing in [Flask](https://flask.palletsprojects.com/en/1.1.x/quickstart/#routing)).
Usually this permits to have more control on the functions to be exposed, compared to using `all` and separate modules.

When comparing decorators and a mapping dictionary, in this context I prefer decorators because it looks cleaner to me. Moreover, using a `feature` decorator allows to enforce some validation, or other pre or post processing, in a similar way to what it would be possible subclassing a base abstract class.

Anyway using decorators is a personal preference, and some web frameworks allows to use either approach. For example in [aiohttp](https://docs.aiohttp.org/en/stable/web_quickstart.html#run-a-simple-web-server) (`app.add_routes([web.get('/', hello)])` vs `@routes.get('/')`)

One requirement noted in #10240 is that we expect users' selections to be retained as they go back through the forms.

With this implementation, we may need to also maintain the values of particular pieces of context, conditionally.

For example on the first page - the default selection is "On my own". If you click "Group learning" -> Continue -> Go back then the default selection is shown, rather than what the user selected originally.

This isn't a huge UX issue (IMO) for that particular example -- but once the user gets into the "Full Device Creation" flow, they'll be going through the handful of questions and if they go back to re-read something then they shouldn't have their selections reset.

The complication comes in because, particularly in the "BACK_SKIP_FACILITY_FORM" event where we run the `revertFullDeviceImport`, we are resetting the selected device and facility. These context properties are used across multiple flows -- so if a user selects a device and facility to import from for a _full device_ - but realizes they really meant to do _learn only_ - then their previous selection would be automatically kept.

Now that I write that out, it actually seems like it might be best not to clear it out because why would they select the wrong device+facility in one flow, go to the other and do a different flow?

---

In any case, it seems that whether we use history and just pop back to the last one, we then will have to manually maintain the context -- except in the specific cases where we deem it necessary to just go back to where we were context and all (ie, if we want to clear out the user's selected devices when they go back from an import without having already imported anything).

I wonder if we could just have `BACK` and something like `BACK_WITH_CONTEXT` where the first does what it does here, then the latter could apply the context of the _current_ state to the target state's context. 

The pattern I used whereby I only set the context whenever the user clicks "continue" means that the previous state will always nullify the context that the transition to the current state set a value to. So one way or the other, we need to be able to maintain previous context when going back.

While reviewing this, the API for ignoring changes is not very good and it seems you're rewriting the whole thing anyway and I have a suggestion for a potentially cleaner approach.

`Reset()` makes sense in the context of Diff/IgnoreChanges, but without the context of the caller, it resembles `Overlay` or `SetAll` which is a more commonly known operation for maps.

With that said, it makes more sense to me to do `PropertyMap.OverlayPath(path, new)` than `PropertyPath.Reset(old, new)`.

This would resolve the issues that:
- `inputs` is expected to get reassigned in callers
- the arguments would be less homogenous so we don't run into argument ordering mistakes

Thoughts?

Hi - thanks for submitting this.

I am reluctant to include it (and #1468) because there is a risk it could break existing implementations.  There are implementations out there which don't use the Admin interface at all.  They just use the library for programmatic imports.  These implementations may be catching and handling the exceptions raised by `widgets.py`, so if we catch and re-raise the exception as `ValueError` this could potentially break these implementations.

I understand there is an issue with these exceptions not being rendered nicely in the UI, but IMO that is something that the UI or control layer should handle.  The lower level implementations need not care about that.

There has been some discussion in the past about how to render the error messages, which can be done with an override (https://github.com/django-import-export/django-import-export/issues/1257#issuecomment-952276485)

I'm not sure this is what we want per default.

For instance, if a Tune run is stopped and I restore and want to continue it, my training will completely fail and it will be impossible for me to resume at all, as the wandb IDs already exist.

I think this setting is trying to solve a problem for the wrong reasons. The original issue was that duplicate trial IDs were generated when starting parallel new experiments, leading to conflicts. We've since improved the trial generation (#29900) so the problem shouldn't come up anymore. And if it did, the correct recommendation here should be that the user should just pass `resume="never"` to their wandb config.


Let's consider the journeys and requirements real quick.

1. I start a run. I expect each run to be logged separately to wandb.

This generally worked and was improved by #29900. For additional guarantees, users can choose to pass `resume="never"`.

2. I start and stop a run. I resume the run. I expect the resumed run to be logged to wandb.

This works at the moment because we just overwrite existing runs per default. With the current PR, this wouldn't work anymore, users would have to pass `resume=None` manually. But this is a far more common case than the problem we encountered in 1.

3. I start and stop a run. I resume the run. I expect the resumed run to be logged to wandb but _not to lose previously logged data_.

This is a bit more tricky, as we may resume from an earlier point in time, so the results in wandb could look a bit funky. I think it may be worthwhile exploring this option. However, so far we haven't seen any user requests for this.

To look into this, we should probably try it out ourselves - how do the results look like with stopping/resuming at the moment (and overwriting) - does this only overwrite local results or also online results? What is the difference if we stop/resume with `resume="auto"`?

Generally I think the current default is sensible as it covers the most common use cases gracefully and we didn't run into problems with the resume behavior so far (other than what we fixed in #29900). But very open to discussions here.
